content:
# Final Review Votes
**Producer:** agent:codex/gpt-5.3-codex-spark-xhigh

## Summary

I recommend accepting the two liveness/ownership correctness fixes and rejecting the immediate missing-PID recovery override. The first two amendments close concrete correctness/safety gaps around stale resources and process identity, while the third sacrifices an existing positive-liveness signal and is safer to keep as a conservative fallback unless a stronger explicit override is added.

## Votes

- `fr-7-45babe55`: Accept
  This addresses a real stale-lock wedge: a run can transition to resumable `failed/paused` while the CLI still holds a writer lease guard and dies before cleanup. Restricting stale-owner reclaim to `running` misses this transition and can permanently block resume with `ProjectWriterLockHeld`, so extending recovery scope improves correctness and operator recoverability without weakening live-owner safety.
- `fr-7-cee54496`: Accept
  Using PID liveness alone when `proc_start_ticks` is missing is unreliable, especially with PID reuse. It can misidentify an unrelated process as the orchestrator, impacting both status/recovery and non-Linux stop behavior, which may terminate the wrong process. Treating missing start-time as stale/unsafe (or adding equivalent platform checks) is a clear safety hardening change.
- `fr-7-81e934dc`: Reject
  This proposal optimizes availability but can introduce false-positive reclamation. A fresh writer lease on missing `run.pid` is currently the only trustworthy indicator for mixed-version/legacy owners; forcing immediate stale classification would race against legitimate in-progress legacy daemons/CLI instances. The 5-minute TTL is a deliberate safety delay rather than a correctness defect unless a stronger explicit override or alternative liveness proof is added.
